verbatim_text: Here, as I mentioned, you're modeling the loss as a function of the number of parameters and the number of tokens. And so here you have three different terms. You have some power loss function that you need to fit in terms of the model size. You add in a power law in terms of the data set size, and then you have this e term. E is just, it absorbs all of the other decisions that you made during pre-training. It sort of says that if you scaled up your model size to infinity and your data set size to infinity, that's the best possible loss you can get. And what we do is we add a third term to this, which is a power law in terms of k, the number of repeated samples. And we jointly optimize all three of these terms. Now we actually have another version of this where we model these three variables, not in terms of the loss, but in terms of the accuracy, the pass at k rate. This is a somewhat more complex scaling law. So I won't go into the details of how this one is actually derived, but it comes from roughly the pass at k function, and we arrive at it using a sort of beta regression problem that relates it to the Chinchilla scaling. Okay, so our scaling laws suggest that training decisions need to account for reasoning. This is what things look like, once again, when you don't take reasoning into account. You have these gray points and this black line here is basically just the pre-training Chinchilla scaling.
